blocks.content: The text came on a Thursday morning to a number he'd saved but never used.
He was at the paint table when the message appeared — the unfamiliar name, the careful phrasing of someone who wasn't sure how they'd be received.
Hi. This is Maya. Cassandra's sister. I got your number from her phone — she knows I have it, but she doesn't know I'm texting you. I hope that's okay.
I know this is probably a strange message to receive. I'd like to meet you. Just to talk. Not with Cassandra — just us. I know how that sounds.
He was quiet for a moment.
It sounds like you have something you need to say to me.
Yes. I do. I'd rather say it in person. Would you be willing?
He typed two different responses, deleted them both.
Yes. Tell me when and where.
The Copper Mug. Tomorrow morning, 9am. If that works.
He sat with the phone for a while after, looking at the exchange. Then he picked up the brush and went back to work, and tried not to build too much out of the shape of it.
